food_subgroup: Mixed noodle dish with poultry and seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for banh hoi (woven thin rice vermicelli). FOODON_00002473 (rice noodle food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Rice noodles) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for banh hoi with grilled chicken and prawn as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of banh hoi ga is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of woven thin rice vermicelli topped with grilled chicken pieces and crushed prawn with scallion oil.

nutrient_source.title: Rice vermicelli with meat and seafood — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central (cooked rice noodles, grilled chicken breast, cooked shrimp) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for banh hoi ga exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 55% cooked rice vermicelli (USDA FDC #169730), 30% grilled chicken breast (USDA FDC #171477), 10% cooked shrimp (USDA FDC #175180), and 5% scallion oil dressing. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this dish as served in a Singapore Vietnamese restaurant context.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to banh hoi (thin rice vermicelli)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Banh hoi is a very thin, woven form of rice vermicelli. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 53 for boiled rice noodles (white rice flour base). This is used as the closest analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (53 × 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g serving) / 100 = 32.6 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 53 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 32.6 per full serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of approximately 175 g to keep the glycemic load below 15 and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat with lean protein sources
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean grilled chicken and prawn with relatively low saturated fat (approximately 0.9 g per 100g); the primary cardiovascular concern is sodium from fish sauce, which should be moderated in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banh Hoi Ga (Bánh Hỏi Gà) is a traditional Vietnamese dish consisting of banh hoi — ultra-thin, delicately woven sheets of steamed rice vermicelli — served with grilled chicken (ga nuong) and crushed or pounded prawn, finished with fragrant scallion oil (mo hanh). The rice noodle sheets have a soft, slightly firm, silky texture that distinguishes them from regular rice vermicelli. The grilled chicken contributes smoky, savoury flavour and lean protein, while the crushed prawn adds umami depth and a subtle sweetness. Scallion oil provides aromatic richness. The dish is typically accompanied by fresh herbs, dipping fish sauce (nuoc cham), and sometimes pickled vegetables. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant and represents a lighter, protein-forward noodle option. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in calories (approximately 142 kcal per 100g), high in lean protein (~10 g per 100g), low in saturated fat, and moderate in carbohydrates from the rice noodle base. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low GI), though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 32.6. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce seasoning, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, making it suitable for those with gluten intolerance or lactose intolerance.

confidence_reason: No exact match for banh hoi ga found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked rice noodles, grilled chicken breast, and cooked shrimp. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry for banh hoi ga exists. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimate. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data. Trans fat is 0.0 confirmed as negligible in lean grilled chicken and prawn with rice noodle base per USDA FDC component data. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant, Lorong Telok, Singapore (field survey listing). Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification. GI category is low (GI 53 is less than or equal to 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 17.5 × 350) / 10000 = 32.6.
